text: This is a thirty year old female who had a laparoscopic gastric bypass seven years later who presented to an outside hospital with severe abdominal pain. She was found to have a small bowel obstruction with some dilated loops in the left mid abdomen and some fecalization of the small bowel. She was transferred to our service and taken to the OR for a diagnostic laparoscopy.

The surgeon identifies the roux limb and observes an adhesive band a few centimeters past the gastrojejunostomy (GJ), causing moderate compression on the roux limb. The surgeon continues to examine the small bowel, running the roux limb to the jejunojejunostomy (JJ), where a small defect is found and closed, though it is not the cause of the obstruction.

The surgeon runs the common channel of the small bowel, observing fecalization and partially obstructed food mixed with contrast. Distal to this area, the point of obstruction is identified as an adhesive band causing extrinsic compression on the common channel.

The surgeon lyses an adhesive band causing obstruction in the small bowel. The small bowel is then run past the transition point to the terminal ileum, revealing fecalization in the distal small bowel.

Dr. Moasis performs the 'Moasis maneuver,' gently milking the fecalized contents of the common channel towards the terminal ileum. He carefully opens the jaws of the instrument to apply just enough pressure to move the intraluminal contents distally without causing serosal injuries, repeating the maneuver several times to decompress the small bowel.

It's important to check and make sure there's no serosal tears as you try this maneuver. And the patient post-operatively had zero pain and tolerated her diet quickly and was discharged within twelve hours.
Thank you for watching the video and I appreciate any comments.
